content.full_text:
California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
David A. Quinn, Manager 
Political Reform Audit Division 
Franchise Tax Board 
9750 Business Park Dr., Ste. 214 
Sacramento, 
CA 
95827 
Dear Mr. Quinn: 
october 28, 1985 
Re: 
Your Request For Advice 
Our File No. A-85-208 
Your File No. 344:DAQ:rk 
This is in response to your memorandum of October 8, 1985, 
requesting advice regarding lobbyist qualification pursuant to the 
Political Reform Act (Government Code Sections 81009-91015). 
Specifically, you asked how to apply the "compensation test" 
contained in FPPC regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18239(b) to 
an attorney employed by a law firm. 
You provided an example in 
which the client of a law firm was billed $3,000 for lobbying 
services for one month. 
The attorney who provided the services 
received $1,400 in compensation attributable to lobbying. 
Regulation Section 18239 states that an individual becomes a 
lobbyist if he or she engages in direct communication with 
qualifying officials for the purpose of influencing legislative or 
administrative action and "receives or becomes entitled to receive 
at least $2,000 in compensation in any calendar month for 
influencing legislative or administrative action." 
Using the above example, you would apply the compensation 
test to the amount of compensation which was received by the 
attorney who provided the lobbying services, not to the amount of 
money received by the law firm. 
Therefore, the attorney in 
question would not qualify as a lobbyist. 
Finally, the exemption from qualification contained in 
section 18239(b) for a full time employee engaged primarily to 
perform services other than lobbying does not apply to attorneys 
employed by a law firm. 
I hope this information is helpful. 
Please let me know if I 
can be of assistance in the future. 
Sincerely, 
{! aA.Lc'-.-~JiLA 
Carla Wardlow 
Political Reform Consultant 
CW:kt 
428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804~0807 • (916) 322~5660 


State of California 
Franchise Tax Board 
Memorandum 
To 
Carla Fardlow 
Consultant, Technical Assistance 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
Date 
: 
October 8, 1985 
File No.: 
344:DAO:rk 
From 
: 
David A. Quinn 
Subject: 
Com pen sat ion T est 
We are requesting your advice on the following situation to 
determine if the individual qualifies as a lobbyist. 
FACTS: 
QUESTIons: 
A lawyer employed by a law firm has been engaged to 
do lobbying work for a client. 
~uring a month, the 
client is billed $3000 for the lobbying services 
performed. 
The lawyer's compensation attributable 
to the lobbying work is $1400 for the same period. 
The lawyer's total compensation per month is $6500. 
What income is to be used for the compensation test 
under regulation section 18239 for a lawyer employed 
by a law firm? 
1. 
Is it the compensation the lawyer is receiving 
for performing the lobbyine? 
or 
2. 
Is it the income the client is paying the law 
firm for the lobbying service? 
and 
3. 
Is he exempt from the full time employee portion 
of the test? 
If you have any additional questions, please contact Sunny Jung 
or myself. 
David A. Quinn, 
~anager 
Political Reform Audit Unit 
General ~udit Section 
Telephone (916) 369-3052

sections.conclusion: Using the above example, you would apply the compensation test to the amount of compensation which was received by the attorney who provided the lobbying services, not to the amount of money received by the law firm. Therefore, the attorney in question would not qualify as a lobbyist. Finally, the exemption from qualification contained in section 18239(b) for a full time employee engaged primarily to perform services other than lobbying does not apply to attorneys employed by a law firm.
sections.question_synthetic: How should the compensation test under FPPC regulation 18239(b) be applied to an attorney employed by a law firm—should it be based on the attorney's direct compensation for lobbying services or the amount billed to the client, and is the attorney exempt as a full-time employee primarily performing non-lobbying services?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The compensation test should be applied to the individual attorney's direct compensation ($1,400) rather than the amount billed to the client ($3,000), meaning the attorney does not qualify as a lobbyist under the $2,000 monthly threshold. Additionally, the full-time employee exemption does not apply to attorneys employed by law firms.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; Skipped (too short): facts; FACTS has only 0 words; LLM: Document contains both the incoming request memorandum from Franchise Tax Board and the FPPC response letter. Both are included in source material. Parsing notes indicated FACTS section was too short but facts are clearly present in the incoming request. Response letter is well-structured with clear question and conclusion sections.

~uring a month, the 
client is billed $3000 for the lobbying services 
performed. 
The lawyer's compensation attributable 
to the lobbying work is $1400 for the same period.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises that the lobbyist compensation test under regulation 18239(b) should be applied to an individual attorney's direct compensation for lobbying work, not the total amount billed by the law firm to its client. The attorney in the example, receiving $1,400 monthly, would not qualify as a lobbyist and is not entitled to the full-time employee exemption.
